title: Transaction Duration Analysis (ThousandEyes)
dataSources: `index=thousandeyes`, ThousandEyes OTel Tests Stream ŌĆö Metrics (Transaction tests)
spl:
`stream_index` thousandeyes.test.type="web-transactions"
| timechart span=5m avg(web.transaction.duration) as avg_transaction_s by thousandeyes.test.name
description: Measures end-to-end time for complex user workflows. Slow transactions directly impact user productivity and satisfaction. Trending reveals gradual degradation across the multi-step flow.
value: Measures end-to-end time for complex user workflows. Slow transactions directly impact user productivity and satisfaction. Trending reveals gradual degradation across the multi-step flow.
implementation: The OTel metric `web.transaction.duration` reports total transaction execution time in seconds (only reported when the transaction completes without errors). The Splunk App Application dashboard includes a "Transaction Duration (s)" line chart with permalink drilldown to ThousandEyes. ThousandEyes also supports OpenTelemetry traces for transaction tests, providing detailed span-level timing.
visualization: Line chart (transaction duration over time), Table (test, agent, duration), Drilldown to ThousandEyes trace view.
knownFalsePositives: Transaction duration stretches when third parties add pixels, consent banners, or when backends scale cold.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
ŌĆó Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Cisco ThousandEyes App for Splunk` (Splunkbase 7719).
ŌĆó Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=thousandeyes`, ThousandEyes OTel Tests Stream ŌĆö Metrics (Transaction tests).
ŌĆó For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ŌĆö Configure data collection
The OTel metric `web.transaction.duration` reports total transaction execution time in seconds (only reported when the transaction completes without errors). The Splunk App Application dashboard includes a "Transaction Duration (s)" line chart with permalink drilldown to ThousandEyes. ThousandEyes also supports OpenTelemetry traces for transaction tests, providing detailed span-level timing.

Step 2 ŌĆö Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
`stream_index` thousandeyes.test.type="web-transactions"
| timechart span=5m avg(web.transaction.duration) as avg_transaction_s by thousandeyes.test.name
```

Understanding this SPL

**Transaction Duration Analysis (ThousandEyes)** ŌĆö Measures end-to-end time for complex user workflows. Slow transactions directly impact user productivity and satisfaction. Trending reveals gradual degradation across the multi-step flow.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=thousandeyes`, ThousandEyes OTel Tests Stream ŌĆö Metrics (Transaction tests). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Cisco ThousandEyes App for Splunk` (Splunkbase 7719). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedŌĆörename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

ŌĆó Invokes macro `stream_index` ŌĆö in Search, use the UI or expand to inspect the underlying SPL.
ŌĆó `timechart` plots the metric over time using **span=5m** buckets with a separate series **by thousandeyes.test.name** ŌĆö ideal for trending and alerting on this use case.


Step 3 ŌĆö Validate
Compare the same tests and time window in the Cisco ThousandEyes App for Splunk dashboard or the test view at app.thousandeyes.com so SplunkŌĆÖs metrics and states match the vendor. If they disagree, check streaming or HEC inputs, macros, and API or token health before retuning.

Step 4 ŌĆö Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (transaction duration over time), Table (test, agent, duration), Drilldown to ThousandEyes trace view.
grandmaExplanation: We time each step in a flow, so we can tell which hop in a chain is dragging without opening ten dashboards.
